full: Abu Bakr Muhammad al-Kalabadhi al-Bukhari
primaryTitle: Systematizer of Early Sufi Doctrine
titles: Systematizer of Early Sufi Doctrine · Theologian of Khorasan

names_i18n.ja: アブー・バクル・ムハンマド・カラーバーズィー

bio: Abu Bakr al-Kalabadhi, in full, Abu Bakr ibn Abi Ishaq Muhammad ibn Ibrahim ibn Ya‘qub al-Bukhari al-Kalabadhi was a Persian Hanafi Maturidi Sufi scholar and the author of the Kitab at-ta'arruf, one of the most important works of Sufism composed during the first 300 years of Islam.

full: Aisha bint Abi Bakr al-Siddiq
primaryTitle: Umm al-Mu'minin — Mother of the Believers
titles: Umm al-Mu'minin · al-Siddiqa

names_i18n.ja: アーイシャ

bio: Aisha bint Abi Bakr was the third and youngest wife of Islamic prophet Muhammad. After Muhammad's death, she was politically active during the Rashidun Caliphate and stands out as a prominent female figure of the period.

full: Abu Muhammad al-Husayn ibn Mas'ud al-Baghawi al-Shafi'i
primaryTitle: Muhyi al-Sunna — Reviver of the Sunnah
titles: Muhyi al-Sunna · Rukn al-Din

names_i18n.ja: アル＝バガウィー

bio: Abū Muḥammad al-Ḥusayn ibn Masʻūd ibn Muḥammad al-Farrā' al-Baghawī, also known as al-Baghawī was a Persian Sunni Muslim scholar based in Khorasan. He was a prominent Quran exegete (mufassir), traditionist (muhaddith), and Shafi'i jurist (faqih). He best known for his two major works, Maʻālim at-Tanzīl and Masabih as-Sunnah.

full: Abu Nasr Muhammad ibn Muhammad al-Farabi al-Turki
primaryTitle: al-Mu'allim al-Thani — The Second Teacher
titles: al-Mu'allim al-Thani (The Second Teacher, after Aristotle) · Father of Islamic Political Philosophy

names_i18n.ja: ファーラービー

full: Abu Hamid Muhammad ibn Muhammad al-Tusi al-Ghazali
primaryTitle: Hujjat al-Islam — Proof of Islam
titles: Hujjat al-Islam (Proof of Islam) · Mujaddid of the 5th Century AH · Zayn al-Din

names_i18n.ja: ガザーリー

bio: Al-Ghazali, (Persian: ابو حامد محمد ابن محمد غزالی توسی, romanized: Abū Ḥāmid Muḥammad ibn Muḥammad Ghazālī Ṭūsi, Latinized as Algazelus, was a Shafi'i Sunni Muslim Persian scholar and polymath. He is known as one of the most prominent and influential jurisconsults, legal theoreticians, muftis, philosophers, theologians, logicians and mystics in Islamic history.

full: Jalal al-Din 'Abd al-Rahman ibn Abi Bakr al-Suyuti al-Shafi'i al-Misri
primaryTitle: Jalal al-Din — The Last Great Classical Scholar
titles: Jalal al-Din · Mujaddid of the 9th century AH · Wrote over 600 works across all Islamic sciences

names_i18n.ja: スユーティー

bio: Jalal al-Din al-Suyuti, or al-Suyuti, was an Egyptian Sunni Muslim polymath of Persian descent. Considered the mujtahid and mujaddid of the Islamic 10th century, he was a leading muhaddith, mufassir, faqīh (jurist), usuli, sufi (mystic), theologian, grammarian, linguist, rhetorician, philologist, lexicographer and historian, who authored works in virtually every Islamic science. For this reason, he was honoured one of the most prestigious and rarest titles: Shaykh al-Islām.

full: Abu Ja'far Muhammad ibn Jarir al-Tabari al-Amuli
primaryTitle: Imam al-Mufassirin — The Imam of Quranic Commentary
titles: Imam al-Mufassirin (Imam of Quranic Commentators) · Independent Mujtahid

names_i18n.zh: 穆罕默德·伊本·贾里尔·塔巴里
names_i18n.ja: タバリー

bio: Abū Jaʿfar Muḥammad ibn Jarīr ibn Yazīd al-Ṭabarī, commonly known as al-Ṭabarī, was a Sunni Muslim scholar, polymath, historian, exegete, jurist, and theologian from Amol, Tabaristan, present-day Iran. Among the most prominent figures of the Islamic Golden Age, al-Tabari is widely known for his historical works and expertise in Quranic exegesis, and has been described as "an impressively prolific polymath". He authored works on a diverse range of subjects, including world history, poetry, lexicography, grammar, ethics, mathematics, and medicine. Among his most famous and influential works are his Quranic commentary, Tafsir al-Tabari, and historical chronicle, Tarikh al-Tabari.

full: Ali ibn Abi Talib ibn Abd al-Muttalib al-Hashimi
primaryTitle: Asad Allah al-Ghalib — Lion of God; Fourth Caliph
titles: Asad Allah al-Ghalib · Amir al-Muminin · Mawla al-Muttaqin

names_i18n.ja: アリー・イブン・アビー・ターリブ

bio: Ali ibn Abi Talib was the fourth Rashidun caliph who ruled from 656 CE until his assassination in 661, as well as the first Shia Imam. He was the cousin and son-in-law of the Islamic prophet Muhammad. Born to Abu Talib ibn Abd al-Muttalib and Fatima bint Asad, Ali was raised in the household of his cousin Muhammad and was among the first to accept his teachings.

full: Abu 'Abd Allah Muhammad ibn Umar Fakhr al-Din al-Razi
primaryTitle: Ibn al-Khatib — The Great Encyclopaedist
titles: Fakhr al-Din (Pride of the Faith) · Imam al-Mushakkikin · Sheikh al-Islam

names_i18n.zh: 法庫魯定 阿爾-拉只
names_i18n.ja: ファフルッディーン・アッ＝ラーズィー

bio: Fakhr al-Dīn al-Rāzī or Fakhruddin Razi, often known by the sobriquet Sultan of the Theologians, was an Islamic polymath, scientist, one of the pioneers of inductive logic and author of Tafsir al-Kabir. He wrote various works in the fields of medicine, chemistry, physics, astronomy, cosmology, literature, theology, ontology, philosophy, history and jurisprudence. He was one of the earliest proponents and skeptics that came up with the concept of multiverse, and compared it with the astronomical teachings of the Quran. A rejector of the geocentric model and the Aristotelian notions of a single universe revolving around a single world, al-Razi argued about the existence of the outer space beyond the known world.

full: Fatima bint Muhammad al-Fihri al-Qurashiyya
primaryTitle: Umm al-Banin — Founder of Al-Qarawiyyin
titles: Umm al-Banin (Mother of the Children) · Founder of the World's First University
bio: Fatima bint Muhammad al-Fihriya al-Qurashiyya, known in shorter form as Fatima al-Fihriya or Fatima al-Fihri, was an Arab woman who is credited with founding the al-Qarawiyyin Mosque in 857–859 CE in Fez, Morocco. She is also known as Umm al-Banīn. Al-Fihriya died around 880 CE. The al-Qarawiyyin Mosque subsequently developed into a teaching institution, which became the modern University of al-Qarawiyyin in 1963. Her story is told by Ibn Abi Zar' in The Garden of Pages  as founder of the mosque. Since she was first mentioned many centuries after her death, her story has been hard to substantiate and some modern historians doubt her existence.

full: Fāṭimah bint Muḥammad ibn ʿAbdillāh al-Hāshimiyya
primaryTitle: al-Zahrāʾ — The Radiant One
titles: al-Zahrāʾ (The Radiant) · al-Baṭūl (The Pure) · Umm Abīhā (Mother of Her Father) · Sayyidat Nisāʾ al-ʿĀlamīn (Lady of the Women of the Worlds) · al-Ṣiddīqa
source: Ibn Saʿd — Ṭabaqāt al-Kubrā · al-Dhahabī — Siyar Aʿlām al-Nubalāʾ · al-Ṭabarī — Tārīkh al-Rusul wa-l-Mulūk

names_i18n.ja: ファーティマ

bio: Fatima bint Muhammad, commonly known as Fatima al-Zahra', was the daughter of the Islamic prophet Muhammad and his wife Khadija. Fatima's husband was Ali, the fourth of the Rashidun caliphs and the first Shia imam. Fatima's sons were Hasan and Husayn, the second and third Shia imams, respectively. Fatima has been compared to Mary, mother of Jesus, especially in Shia Islam. Muhammad is said to have regarded her as the best of women and the dearest person to him. She is often viewed as an ultimate archetype for Muslim women and an example of compassion, generosity, and enduring suffering. It is through Fatima that Muhammad's family line has survived to this date. Her name and her epithets remain popular choices for Muslim girls.

full: Harun ibn Muhammad al-Mahdi ibn al-Mansur al-Abbasi
primaryTitle: Amir al-Muminin — Caliph of the Golden Age
titles: Amir al-Muminin · Harun al-Rashid

names_i18n.ja: ハールーン・アッ＝ラシード

bio: Abū Jaʿfar Hārūn ibn Muḥammad ar-Rashīd, or simply Hārūn ibn al-Mahdī, famously known as Hārūn al-Rashīd, was the fifth Abbasid caliph of the Abbasid Caliphate, reigning from September 786 until his death in March 809. His reign is traditionally regarded to be the beginning of the Islamic Golden Age. His epithet al-Rashid translates to "the Just", "the Upright", or "the Rightly-Guided".

full: Abu Sa'id al-Hasan ibn Abi al-Hasan Yasar al-Basri
primaryTitle: Sayyid al-Tabi'in
titles: Sayyid al-Tabi'in · Imam al-Basra · Fakhr al-Umma

names_i18n.ja: ハサン・アル＝バスリー

bio: Abi Sa'id al-Hasan ibn Yasar al-Basri, often referred to as al-Hasan al-Basri, was an early Medieval Muslim preacher, ascetic, theologian, exegete, scholar, and judge.

full: Shams al-Din Abu 'Abd Allah Muhammad Ibn Qayyim al-Jawziyya
primaryTitle: Ibn Qayyim al-Jawziyya — Scholar of the Heart
titles: Ibn Qayyim al-Jawziyya · Closest student of Ibn Taymiyya · Imprisoned alongside his teacher

names_i18n.ja: イブン・カイイム・アルジャウズィー

bio: Shams ad-Dīn ʾAbū ʿAbd Allāh Muḥammad ibn ʾAbī Bakr ibn ʾAyyūb az-Zurʿī ad-Dimashqī al-Ḥanbalī, commonly known as Ibn Qayyim al-Jawziyyah or Ibn al-Qayyim for short, or reverentially as al-Imam Ibn al-Qayyim in Sunni tradition, was an important medieval Syrian Arab Islamic jurist, theologian, and spiritual writer. Belonging to the Hanbali school of Fiqh, of which he is regarded as "one of the most important thinkers," Ibn al-Qayyim was also the foremost disciple and student of Ibn Taymiyyah, with whom he was imprisoned in 1326 for dissenting against established tradition during Ibn Taymiyyah's famous incarceration in the Citadel of Damascus.

full: Abu 'Abd Allah Muhammad Muhyi al-Din ibn 'Arabi al-Andalusi
primaryTitle: Shaykh al-Akbar — The Greatest Master
titles: Shaykh al-Akbar (The Greatest Master) · Muhyi al-Din · al-Kibrit al-Ahmar (The Red Sulphur)

names_i18n.ja: イブン・アル＝アラビー

bio: Ibn 'Arabī was a Sunni Muslim Arab scholar, Sufi mystic, poet, and Muslim philosopher from al-Andalus, who exercised notable influence within Sufi metaphysics and Islamic thought in general. There are 850 works attributed to Ibn 'Arabi, though only 700 of these are considered authentic, and only 400 are extant. His cosmological teachings became a dominant intellectual framework in many regions of the Muslim world.

full: Isma'il ibn Umar ibn Kathir al-Dimashqi
primaryTitle: Imam al-Tafsir — Master of Quranic Exegesis
titles: Hafiz al-Hadith · Imam al-Mufassirin

names_i18n.ja: イブン・カスィール・ディマシュキー

bio: Abu al-Fida Isma'il ibn Umar ibn Kathir al-Dimashqi, known simply as Ibn Kathir, was an Arab Islamic exegete, historian and scholar. An expert on tafsir, tarikh (history) and fiqh, he is considered a leading authority on Sunni Islam.

full: Abu Zayd 'Abd al-Rahman ibn Muhammad ibn Khaldun al-Tunisi
primaryTitle: Father of Sociology and Modern Historiography
titles: Father of Sociology · Father of Modern Historiography · Inventor of the science of 'Asabiyyah

names_i18n.ms: Abdul Al-Rahman Ibn Muhammad Ibn Khaldun
names_i18n.ja: イブン・ハルドゥーン

full: Abu 'Ali al-Husayn ibn 'Abd Allah ibn Sina al-Bukhari
primaryTitle: al-Shaykh al-Ra'is — Prince of Physicians
titles: al-Shaykh al-Ra'is (Prince Among the Learned) · Avicenna (Latin) · Hujjat al-Haqq (Proof of the Truth)

names_i18n.ja: イブン・スィーナー

bio: Ibn Sina, commonly known in the West as Avicenna, was a preeminent philosopher and physician of the Muslim world. He was a seminal figure of the Islamic Golden Age, serving in the courts of various Iranian rulers, and was influential to medieval European medical and Scholastic thought.

full: Taqi al-Din Ahmad ibn 'Abd al-Halim ibn Taymiyya al-Harrani
primaryTitle: Shaykh al-Islam — The Controversial Reformer
titles: Shaykh al-Islam · Imam al-Mujtahid · Imprisoned 4 times by Mamluk authorities

names_i18n.ja: イブン・タイミーヤ

bio: Ibn Taymiyya was a Sunni Muslim scholar, jurist, Mujtahid, traditionist, Qadiri Sufi, proto-Salafi theologian and iconoclast. Born in Harran in 1263 CE and fleeing from the Mongol invasion, he was taught by his grandfather and father in the principles of Islamic Jurisprudence at Damascus. Clerics and state authorities accused Ibn Taymiyya and his disciples of anthropomorphism, which eventually led to the censoring of his works and subsequent incarceration.

full: Abu Hanifa al-Nu'man ibn Thabit al-Kufi
primaryTitle: al-Nu'man — Imam al-A'zam, The Greatest Imam
titles: Imam al-A'zam (The Greatest Imam) · Siraj al-Umma · Father of Hanafi School

names_i18n.ja: アブー・ハニーファ

bio: Abu Hanifa was a Muslim scholar, jurist, theologian, ascetic, and eponym of the Hanafi school of Sunni jurisprudence, which remains the most widely practiced to this day. His school predominates in Central and South Asia, Turkey, Africa, the Balkans, Russia, and some parts of the Arab world.

full: Abu 'Abd Allah Ahmad ibn Muhammad ibn Hanbal al-Shaybani
primaryTitle: Imam Ahl al-Sunna — The Imam Who Refused to Capitulate
titles: Imam Ahl al-Sunna wa'l-Jama'a · Sayyid al-Muhaddithun · Imprisoned and flogged rather than accept Mu'tazili theology

full: Abu 'Abd Allah Muhammad ibn Ismail al-Bukhari
primaryTitle: Amir al-Mu'minin fi'l-Hadith — Prince of Hadith
titles: Amir al-Mu'minin fi'l-Hadith (Prince of the Faithful in Hadith) · Imam al-Muhaddithun
bio: The most celebrated hadith scholar in Sunni Islam and compiler of Sahih al-Bukhari, widely regarded as the most authentic hadith collection after the Quran. He memorized over 600,000 hadith and traveled extensively to collect traditions. Exiled from Nishapur over a theological dispute.
tribe_or_lineage: al-Ju'fi (mawla); Persian descent, earliest ancestor Bardizbah was Zoroastrian
key_events: Exiled from Nishapur over theological dispute
Traveled throughout the Islamic world collecting hadith
reliability_grade: Imam, Amir al-Mu'minin fi al-Hadith (highest grade)
full_name_ar: أبو عبد الله محمد بن إسماعيل بن إبراهيم الجعفي البخاري

names_i18n.zh: 穆罕默德·伊本·伊斯梅尔·布哈里
names_i18n.ja: ムハンマド・アル＝ブハーリー

full: Abu 'Abd Allah Malik ibn Anas al-Asbahi al-Madani
primaryTitle: Imam Dar al-Hijra — Imam of Medina
titles: Imam Dar al-Hijra (Imam of the City of Migration) · Imam al-Hijaz

names_i18n.ja: マーリク・イブン・アナス

bio: Malik ibn Anas, also known as Imam Malik, was a Muslim scholar, jurist, muhaddith and traditionalist who is the eponym of the Maliki school, one of the four schools of Islamic jurisprudence in Sunni Islam.

full: Abu al-Husayn Muslim ibn al-Hajjaj al-Qushayri al-Naysaburi
primaryTitle: Imam al-Sahih — Student of al-Bukhari
titles: Imam al-Sahih · Student of al-Bukhari · Imam of Nishapur

names_i18n.ja: ムスリム・イブン・ハッジャージュ

bio: Abū al-Ḥusayn Muslim ibn al-Ḥajjāj ibn Muslim ibn Ward al-Qushayrī an-Naysābūrī, commonly known as Imam Muslim, was an Islamic scholar from the city of Nishapur, particularly known as a muhaddith. His hadith collection, known as Sahih Muslim, is one of the six major hadith collections in Sunni Islam and is regarded as one of the two most authentic (sahih) collections, alongside Sahih al-Bukhari.

full: Muhammad ibn 'Abd Allah al-Hashimi al-Qurashi
primaryTitle: Seal of the Prophets · Mercy to the Worlds · al-Mustafa
titles: Rasul Allah · al-Mustafa · al-Amin · al-Sadiq · Shafi al-Mudhnibeen · Rahmatul lil Alameen · Habib Allah · Khayru l-Khalq · Sayyid al-Mursalin · al-Nabi al-Ummi

quranRef.epithet: Seal of the Prophets · The Praised One · Mercy to the Worlds

dateNote: Born in the Year of the Elephant (Aam al-Fil). Dates follow the Islamic historical tradition.
dating_source: Ibn Ishaq Sirat Rasul Allah (via Ibn Hisham); al-Tabari 'History'; multiple contemporary and near-contemporary attestations
dating_note: Fully historical. Born in Mecca 570 CE (Year of the Elephant); died in Medina 8 June 632 CE. Dates firmly established in Islamic and non-Islamic sources.

names_i18n.ja: ムハンマド・イブン＝アブドゥッラーフ

full: Salah al-Din Yusuf ibn Ayyub al-Kurdi
primaryTitle: Al-Nasir — Defender of the Faith; Liberator of Jerusalem
titles: Al-Nasir Salah al-Din · Sultan al-Islam

names_i18n.ja: サラーフッディーン

bio: Salah ad-Din Yusuf ibn Ayyub, commonly known as Saladin, was a Kurdish commander and political leader. He was the founder of the Ayyubid dynasty and the first sultan of both Egypt and Syria. An important figure of the Third Crusade, he spearheaded the Muslim military effort against the Crusader states in the Levant. At the height of his power, the Ayyubid realm spanned Egypt, Syria, Upper Mesopotamia, the Hejaz, Yemen, and Nubia.

full: Sharaf al-Din 'Umar ibn 'Ali ibn al-Farid al-Hamawi al-Misri
primaryTitle: Sultan al-'Ashiqin — Sultan of Lovers
titles: Sultan al-'Ashiqin (Sultan of Lovers) · Mawla al-'Arifin

full: Umar ibn al-Khattab ibn Nufayl al-Qurashi
primaryTitle: Al-Faruq — The Distinguisher; Second Caliph
titles: Al-Faruq · Amir al-Muminin · Umar al-Akbar

names_i18n.ur: عمر ابن الخطاب رضی اللہ تعالٰی عنہ
names_i18n.ja: ウマル・イブン・ハッターブ

full: Uthman ibn Affan ibn Abi al-As al-Qurashi
primaryTitle: Dhul-Nurayn — Possessor of Two Lights; Third Caliph
titles: Dhul-Nurayn · Amir al-Muminin

names_i18n.ja: ウスマーン・イブン・アッファーン

bio: Uthman ibn Affan was the third caliph of the Rashidun Caliphate, ruling from 644 until his assassination in 656. Uthman, a second cousin, son-in-law, and senior companion of the Islamic prophet Muhammad, played a major role in early Islamic history. During his reign as caliph, he was known for ordering the official compilation of the standardized version of the Quran, known as the Uthmanic codex, which is still used today.

full: Abu Zakariya Yahya ibn Sharaf al-Nawawi
primaryTitle: Muhyi al-Din — Reviver of Religion
titles: Muhyi al-Din · Imam al-Nawawi
